food_subgroup: Phyllo pastry with nut filling soaked in sugar syrup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for baklava exists; FOODON_03311531 is the closest defensible parent node for layered phyllo pastry.
ontology.foodex2_description: Baklava phyllo pastry with nuts and sugar syrup
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for baklava as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard diamond-cut or square-cut piece of baklava weighing approximately 40 grams, as typically served at Turkish or Lebanese restaurants or pastry shops.

nutrient_source.title: Baklava — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID: 168917)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy entry for baklava. Values represent a composite of traditional Turkish/Lebanese baklava made with phyllo dough, mixed nuts (pistachios or walnuts), clarified butter, and sugar syrup. All per-100g values are taken directly from this approved source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sucrose-Based Confections and Phyllo Pastry — analogous estimate anchored to sucrose GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for baklava exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated based on the dominant macronutrient driver: sucrose-dominant sugar syrup (sucrose GI ~65, University of Sydney GI Database). The rapid digestion of refined phyllo starch and the high sucrose load push the composite GI to approximately 70 (high). GL per 40g serving = (70 × 55.4 × 0.40) / 100 = 15.4. This estimation methodology follows the analogous-food approach approved under Project Veritas v2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 175 mg/100g (~70 mg per 40g piece); low acute sodium load
hypertension.guidance: A single 40g piece of baklava contributes approximately 70 mg sodium, which is well below the 2,300 mg daily limit; however, the high saturated fat content (3.2 g per piece) and very high sugar load represent secondary cardiovascular risk factors that hypertensive patients should monitor.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~70 (high), GL ~15.4 per piece, ~22 g sugar per 40g serving from sucrose-dominant syrup
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baklava is high-GI (GI ~70) with a glycemic load of approximately 15.4 per standard 40g piece, driven by the sucrose-dominant sugar syrup; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat baklava as an occasional indulgence only, limiting to one piece and pairing with protein or fat to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.0 g/100g (3.2 g per piece from clarified butter) combined with very high added sugar (~22 g per piece)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from clarified butter (3.2 g per 40g piece) and high added sugar (~22 g per piece) elevates LDL cholesterol and triglyceride risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit baklava to rare occasions and consume no more than one piece, while the MUFA and PUFA from pistachio or walnut filling provide a modest offsetting anti-inflammatory benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (22 g per piece) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine release; saturated fat activates TLR4 pathway
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit baklava to rare occasions (at most once monthly) as the combined high sugar and saturated fat load promotes postprandial hyperglycaemia and pro-inflammatory cytokine (IL-6, TNF-α) release, outweighing the modest anti-inflammatory benefit of pistachio or walnut MUFA and PUFA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baklava is a traditional Middle Eastern layered pastry originating from the Ottoman Empire, widely consumed across Turkey, Lebanon, Greece, and the broader Levant region. It is made from many ultra-thin sheets of phyllo (fillo) dough alternating with a filling of finely chopped pistachios or walnuts, bound with clarified butter (ghee), baked until golden and crisp, then immediately soaked in warm sugar syrup made from sucrose, water, and often rose water or orange blossom water. The syrup penetration creates the characteristic sticky, intensely sweet texture. Per 100g: 428 kcal, 5.7g protein, 22.5g fat (8g saturated from butter), 55.4g carbohydrate, 54g sugar, 1.3g dietary fibre, 175mg sodium. Per standard 40g diamond-cut piece: ~171 kcal, ~2.3g protein, ~9g fat, ~22g carbohydrate, ~22g sugar. GI ~70 (high), GL ~15.4 per piece. One piece provides approximately 44% of the WHO daily added-sugar limit (50g/day). Clinically, baklava is a high-GI, high-sugar dessert unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The nut filling provides modest MUFA and PUFA, and pistachios contribute polyphenols, but these benefits are outweighed by the dominant sugar and saturated fat load. Source: USDA SR Legacy. Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy, an approved primary source with an exact baklava entry. GI estimated via analogous-food methodology anchored to University of Sydney GI Database sucrose reference values. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from the approved source. Confidence is high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for baklava is not separately reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry consulted; total fat (22.5g/100g) and saturated fat (8.0g/100g) are confirmed. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the USDA SR Legacy baklava entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — baklava contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D source in traditional baklava). GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose GI reference from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for baklava was found in the database as of the access date. Baklava is a Middle Eastern dish; no mandatory local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, Vietnamese) are required under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules, which apply to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes specifically. Arabic alias forms are included for regional completeness.
